03 · 09

Profile snapshot

Tagline & Bio
"Senior backend engineer building distributed systems for the African fintech wave." Eight years building payment infrastructure across Lagos, Cape Town, and Berlin. Specialized in event-driven architectures, Postgres at scale, and Go services that don't crash at 3 AM. Currently focused on idempotent transaction systems and the boring-but-critical work that keeps money moving.

★★★★★ Studio Berlin GmbH · Apr 2026
Built our payment reconciliation pipeline from zero. Hit deadlines, wrote tests, and explained tradeoffs in plain language during architecture reviews. Would hire again.
★★★★★ Quantum Robotics · Apr 2026
Senior eng you can throw a vague problem at and get back something thoughtful. Caught an idempotency bug in our existing code while building the new feature.

03 · 08

Profile snapshot

About · public-facing
"An independent brand & identity studio in Berlin. We work with companies that build interesting things — most of them, you've heard of." Founded 2018 by two former Pentagram designers. Studio Berlin specializes in identity systems, packaging, and digital product design for fintech, hospitality, and consumer-tech clients across Europe and North America. Notable past work: rebrand of Trade Republic (2021), identity for the Mast restaurant group (2023).
Company details
Industry
Design · Brand & Identity
Headquarters
Berlin, Germany
Company size
12–50 employees
Founded
March 2018
Hiring focus
Engineering, design, and design-engineering hybrids
Repeat hire rate
62% (above platform average of 38%)
Categories hired for
Senior Engineer Brand Designer Frontend Engineer Data Analyst Product Designer Motion Designer
Trust tier earned
Top Client
Earned after 15 successful hires with zero unresolved disputes. Top Client status grants priority shortlists, lower fees, and dedicated specialist attention.
Public reviews from talent · 4.8 avg from 23 hires
★★★★★ Adesuwa Babatunde · Apr 2026
Clear scope, fair rate, fast feedback cycles. Lukas knows what he's doing technically — code reviews are useful, not performative. Would work with again.
★★★★★ Anika Patel · Mar 2026
Best client engagement I've had on Atlas. They paid early once because their accounting was running ahead of schedule. Real respect for craft.

Notes

Last updated 2d ago
Manager's notes Mateo Vargas Manager + Admin

"Daniel is the team's most consistent operator. Worth coaching toward team-lead-style responsibilities — he's already mentoring Lukas C. informally."

Q1 highlights from 1:1s:

  • Wants more exposure to dispute mediation training (signed up for May course)
  • Has expressed interest in eventually moving into recruitment-sprint coordination
  • Caseload above target — discussed with Mateo on Apr 28; rebalancing 5 cases to Lukas C.
  • Excellent rapport with Studio Berlin and other Berlin-area clients · "use this as a model"
Last edited Apr 28, 2026 11:42 AM by Mateo Vargas
Admin's notes Aïsha Okafor (you) Admin-only

HR-side observations from Mar 2026 quarterly check:

  • Compliance training fully current (last completed Apr 5)
  • No HR-flagged behaviors; no peer complaints
  • Manager flagged "consistently above caseload target" — confirmed acceptable as long as SLA holds (it does)
  • Eligible for retention review at end of Q2 per the 3-year tenure milestone
Last edited Mar 12, 2026 by Aïsha Okafor (you)

Manager · Mateo Vargas

05 · 08

Notes

Last updated 4d ago
Admin's notes Aïsha Okafor (you) Admin-only

"Mateo is the most consistent operator on the platform. Five years running, never missed a daily report, never failed an audit cycle. His judgement on caseload and dispute escalations is uniquely well-calibrated."

Q1 observations from quarterly Admin review:

  • Calibration variance with my own ratings was ±0.4 — within excellent band
  • Handles his own personal caseload at 62% capacity, leaving headroom for managerial work — good split
  • Cross-team coordination on the Customer Support pool depletion was textbook — escalated cleanly with full data + proposal attached
  • Watch: he's overdue for an exec-track conversation. Tenure milestone Q3 2026.
Last edited Apr 26, 2026 by Aïsha Okafor (you)
Self-evaluation Mateo Vargas Self + Admin

"My calibration with Admin is tighter this quarter than last. Two areas to keep working on: (1) anticipating pool depletion earlier — Customer Support shouldn't have surprised me, the trend was visible 30 days out, (2) more deliberate succession planning — Daniel is ready for more, I should be giving him concrete stretches."

Submitted as part of Q1 2026 self-evaluation cycle.

Submitted Apr 10, 2026 · auto-shared with Admin Read-only · self-eval window closed

Performance review history

5 reviews on file · all delivered by Admin
Q1 2026 reviewLatest Final · delivered Apr 14, 2026 · cycle annual
Overall rating Exceeds expectations ★★★★★ Audit score 4.6 / 5
Admin · Aïsha Okafor (you)
Five years of remarkable consistency. Mateo's calibration with my own ratings is the tightest on the platform. The Customer Support pool intervention this quarter was textbook escalation — clean data, clear proposal, executed without friction. One growth area: more proactive talent development for his strongest specialists, particularly Daniel.
Super Admin · Dario Mensah
Mateo is one of the platform's most strategic assets. His judgement on edge cases — particularly cross-category coordination — is uniquely good. Recommend formal exec-track conversation by end of Q3. He's ready, and the platform needs people of his caliber moving up.

JOB ACTIONS
01 · 05

Job description

Posted Apr 26 · edited Apr 29 (rate range tightened)
Full posting · client-facing copy
412 words · 2-min read · auto-saved Apr 29 17:42 CET

About the role

Studio Berlin is hiring a Senior Frontend Engineer to lead the rebuild of our client-facing portfolio platform — a Vue 3 / Nuxt 3 SPA with a custom design system, hand-built motion, and a small but opinionated codebase. You'll work directly with our founder Stefan Müller and our backend engineer Adesuwa.

This is a long-term, ongoing engagement (3-month minimum, open-ended after) at 25–30 hours/week. We're flexible on timezone but expect ~4 hours of overlap with Berlin (CET) for sync work.

What you'll do

  • Lead the migration from Vue 2 / Nuxt 2 to Vue 3 / Nuxt 3 (in progress, ~30% complete)
  • Build and maintain a small but precise design system in Vue + Tailwind
  • Collaborate with Adesuwa on the BFF layer (Hono / Drizzle on Cloudflare Workers)
  • Own performance: target Lighthouse ≥95, LCP <1.5s on 4G, zero CLS regressions
  • Ship motion and interaction polish — subtle but considered (think Linear, not stripe.com)

What we're looking for

  • 5+ years of professional frontend experience, with at least 2 years on Vue 3 or Nuxt 3
  • Strong sensibility for editorial / brand-led design — you can take an unfinished comp and finish it
  • Comfort with TypeScript, Tailwind, and modern build tooling (Vite)
  • Experience with motion libraries (GSAP, Motion One, or hand-crafted)
  • Async-friendly · clear writing · self-directed

Nice to have

  • Background in agency or studio work (vs. pure product)
  • Experience with Cloudflare Workers / edge compute
  • A portfolio with at least one project you're genuinely proud of (we'll ask)

Compensation & logistics

Hourly rate
$55–65/h
based on experience · weekly cycle
Hours
25–30/wk
~4h CET overlap required
Commitment
3-month min
open-ended after · 30-day notice

About Studio Berlin

We're a 4-person industrial design and brand studio in Berlin (real legal name: Atelier Werkraum GmbH — Studio Berlin is our trading name). We work with consumer hardware companies, exhibition designers, and a small handful of long-term retainer clients. Our existing engineering team is one full-time backend (Adesuwa, also through Atlas) and Stefan dabbles in code. Quiet, considered, no rush culture.

Specialist deciding Stage 3 · 1 day to SLA opened Apr 24, 2026 · 6d 14h elapsed

Work quality dispute on a fixed-price DevOps engagement

SB Studio Berlin REAL: ATELIER WERKRAUM VS TO Tomás Oliveira · eng-004 · $8,500 fixed-price · $4,250 escrow held
DSP-2026-0144
Claimant
Stefan Müller
Studio Berlin · client
Respondent
Tomás Oliveira
cand-019 · 🇵🇹 Lisbon
Disputed amount
$2,550
claim: 60% milestone refund
Escrow held
$4,250
milestone 2 · paused
Owner
Daniel K.
Engineering specialist
Stage-3 SLA approaching breach.
Daniel has 1 business day to lock his decision before this auto-escalates to admin (you). Decision drafted Apr 30 · awaiting both parties' acknowledgement of proposed split before lock.
DISPUTE LIFECYCLE · 5 STAGES
Currently at Stage 3 · Specialist decision · stages 1–2 cleared without resolution

ADMIN ACTIONS
01 · 06

Dispute submission

Filed by claimant · 6 days 14 hours ago
CLAIMANT Stefan Müller's claim
Filed Apr 24, 2026 14:08 CET · via in-thread Dispute control
SM
Stefan Müller
Founder, Studio Berlin (Atelier Werkraum GmbH) · cl-002

Reason category: Work Quality · most common after Hours Tracking

The DevOps deliverable for Milestone 2 does not meet the specification we agreed in the contract. Tomás was contracted to set up a Kubernetes cluster on AWS with proper IAM scoping, monitoring (Prometheus + Grafana), and CI/CD pipelines for our 4 services. What we received is a working cluster, but:

  • IAM uses a single broad role for all services rather than per-service scoping (security concern)
  • Grafana dashboards are generic Prometheus templates · no service-specific metrics
  • CI/CD only covers 2 of 4 services · Tomás said the remaining 2 were "out of scope" (they were not)

Per the milestone agreement, milestone 2 acceptance criteria explicitly includes "service-scoped IAM" and "service-specific dashboards." I am requesting 60% refund of milestone 2 ($2,550) for the gap between agreed scope and delivered work. I'm willing to pay the remaining 40% ($1,700) for the work that was completed correctly.

"Atlas time-tracking data shows Tomás logged 47 hours on this milestone. The CI/CD half-build alone explains maybe 15-20 of those hours. The remaining IAM and dashboard gaps are not partial-credit issues — they're scope items he chose not to do." — Stefan Müller, dispute submission, Apr 24 14:08 CET
REQUESTED REFUND $2,550 · 60% of milestone 2 escrow
EVIDENCE SUBMITTED · 5 FILES
Milestone-2-spec.pdf
PDF · 142 KB · signed Apr 1
REVIEWED ✓
aws-iam-screenshot.png
PNG · 1.2 MB · captured Apr 23
REVIEWED ✓
grafana-dashboards.png
PNG · 890 KB · captured Apr 23
REVIEWED ✓
slack-thread-export.pdf
PDF · 312 KB · 38 messages
REVIEWED ✓
github-pr-comments.pdf
PDF · 78 KB · 14 comments
REVIEWED ✓
02 · 06

Respondent's response

Filed Apr 25, 2026 · 22h after claim
RESPONDENT Tomás Oliveira's response
Filed Apr 25, 2026 11:42 WET · within Atlas-recommended 24h window
TO
Tomás Oliveira
DevOps Engineer · cand-019 · Lisbon, Portugal

I disagree with Stefan's interpretation of the milestone scope, and the requested 60% refund is excessive. I believe the work delivered substantially meets the agreed milestone, with one legitimate gap that I'm willing to address.

On IAM scoping: The original spec said "appropriate IAM for the cluster," not "per-service IAM." Per-service IAM scoping is a security best practice but requires significant additional work (estimated 12–15h) that wasn't budgeted. I raised this in our Slack on Apr 12 and Stefan agreed to defer it. I have screenshots of that conversation.

On Grafana dashboards: I delivered the four standard Prometheus dashboards (Node, Pods, Deployments, Ingress). The spec said "Grafana setup with monitoring" — service-specific dashboards were never explicitly listed as deliverables. I'm happy to build them but they were not in scope.

On CI/CD coverage: This is the legitimate gap. I covered 2 of 4 services. The spec says "CI/CD for the platform services" without listing all four. I read it as the two main services. I'm willing to credit back $850 (50% of pro-rated time on those 2 services).

"I want to resolve this fairly. I'm not contesting that the CI/CD coverage is incomplete by Stefan's reading. But the IAM and dashboard items were not in the agreed scope, and 60% is asking me to give back work that was actually done correctly. A $850 credit covers the ambiguous portion." — Tomás Oliveira, response, Apr 25 11:42 WET
COUNTER-OFFER $850 · 20% of milestone 2 (CI/CD pro-rata)
RESPONDENT EVIDENCE · 4 FILES
slack-iam-defer-Apr12.png
PNG · 642 KB · 4 messages
REVIEWED ✓
milestone-2-spec-annotated.pdf
PDF · 168 KB · my reading
REVIEWED ✓
time-log-export.csv
CSV · 22 KB · 47h logged
REVIEWED ✓
git-history-summary.pdf
PDF · 94 KB · 38 commits
REVIEWED ✓
03 · 06

Specialist's investigation

Daniel Kovács · 6 days investigating · 14 actions logged
Daniel's investigation workspace
Editable until decision locked · admin can read · candidate + client cannot see
DANIEL'S NOTES ON THE CASE
Apr 30, 2026 · 16:18 CET — Updated. Both parties reviewed at length. After re-reading the milestone-2 spec four times, the truth is genuinely in the middle: On IAM: The spec says "appropriate IAM for the cluster" which is ambiguous. Tomás did raise the per-service-vs-shared distinction in Slack Apr 12, and Stefan did say "let's defer that" — but the deferral was specifically for "per-service IAM with rotation policies" not the broader scoping. I think Tomás reasonably interpreted that as deferring all per-service work, and Stefan reasonably remembers it as deferring only the rotation piece. Genuinely ambiguous. On Grafana dashboards: The spec says "Grafana setup with monitoring." That's ambiguous as written. Industry standard for "monitoring" on a 4-service platform would include service-specific dashboards. But it's not unreasonable to read "monitoring" as just "the four core Prometheus dashboards." Tomás's reading is defensible but on the thin side. On CI/CD: This one is clearer. The spec is genuinely silent on coverage scope. Tomás picking 2 of 4 was a unilateral decision, even if the spec didn't explicitly require all 4. Tomás owes more than $850 here. Recommendation: Split the difference. $1,700 refund to Stefan (40% of milestone 2). $2,550 released to Tomás. This treats CI/CD as Tomás's gap (worth ~$1,200), and the IAM + dashboard ambiguity as a 50/50 split (worth ~$500 to client side).
EVIDENCE REVIEWED · 9 ITEMS
Milestone-2 spec PDF · re-read 3× Apr 25 18:14
AWS IAM screenshots vs. industry baseline Apr 26 10:42
Grafana dashboards — quality assessed Apr 26 14:18
Slack Apr 12 IAM-defer thread · key Apr 27 09:08
Time-tracker logs · 47h breakdown Apr 27 14:42
Git history · 38 commits · file scope Apr 28 11:14
GitHub PR comments · stakeholder review Apr 28 16:48
Slack thread export · 38-msg history Apr 29 10:08
External AWS DevOps consultant 2nd opinion declined · cost
CONVERSATIONS WITH PARTIES
Daniel held 3 calls with each party over the last 6 days · 1× joint mediation call (Apr 28) · 14 messages exchanged in admin-only thread · all calls recorded with consent and stored under DSP-2026-0144 audit trail.
Key conversation excerpts
3 of 142 messages · selected by Daniel as decisional
SM
Stefan Müller Apr 12, 2026 · 14:42 CET FLAGGED · KEY
"Yeah let's defer the per-service IAM with rotation for now — that's a lot of work and we can come back to it. Get the cluster running first."
TO
Tomás Oliveira Apr 12, 2026 · 14:48 CET FLAGGED · KEY
"OK 👍 will use a single IAM role for the cluster for now. Let me know when you want to revisit per-service."
SM
Stefan Müller Apr 18, 2026 · 09:14 CET CI/CD
"Where are the pipelines for the other two services? I assumed all four. Can you also do those before we mark m2 complete?"
04 · 06

Specialist's proposed decision

Drafted Apr 30 · awaiting both-party acknowledgement
Proposed split: 40% client / 60% candidate
DRAFT · NOT LOCKED
CLIENT REFUND
Stefan Müller (Studio Berlin)
−$1,700
40% of milestone 2 escrow refunded · less than the $2,550 requested
CANDIDATE PAYOUT
Tomás Oliveira
+$2,550
60% of milestone 2 escrow released · more than the $850 counter-offer
DANIEL'S RATIONALE
CI/CD coverage is the one clear gap. Tomás chose to cover 2 of 4 services unilaterally, and the spec is silent on scope. That gap is worth approximately $1,200 of the disputed amount.

IAM scoping and Grafana dashboards are genuinely ambiguous. The Apr 12 Slack exchange shows Stefan deferring "per-service IAM with rotation" — a narrower deferral than Tomás interpreted, but a real one. Splitting the remaining $1,000 of disputed amount 50/50 reflects that ambiguity fairly.

Net result: $1,700 refund to Stefan ($1,200 + $500), $2,550 released to Tomás. Both parties get a result they can live with — neither got what they asked for, but neither lost decisively. Decision does not require either party to redo work.
Status: Stefan acknowledged Apr 30 17:42 CET. Tomás's ack pending — last seen 4h ago. Auto-locks at SLA breach (May 1, 14:08 CET) if not contested.

REASONS FOR PENDING ESCALATION
If Tomás contests the proposed decision (he has 36h to do so), the case auto-escalates to Stage 4 (Admin decision) — which lands on your desk. Tomás's $850 counter is significantly below Daniel's $2,550 candidate-side proposal, but Daniel's split arguably gives Tomás more than he asked for ($2,550 vs his $1,700 = $850 counter-implied). Likelihood of escalation: low (≤25%). Stefan has already acknowledged.
